summary: This post traces the evolution of data storage from 1951's UNISERVO tape drives through modern NVMe and emerging technologies like HAMR and storage class memory. Using a single byte (the letter 'j') as a narrative thread, Frazelle explains how each storage medium physically represents 0s and 1s—from holes in paper cards to magnetic polarities to trapped electrons in floating gates. The piece concludes by examining cutting-edge technologies like phase change memory, helium-filled drives, and heat-assisted magnetic recording that will define storage's future.
key_points: Storage has evolved from sequential-access tape (1951) to random-access disk (1956) to modern NVMe drives that are 486,111% faster than original metal tape
The fundamental unit remains the same—storing 0s and 1s—but the physical mechanisms vary wildly: holes in cards, magnetic polarities, electron positions, phase states, and optical reflectivity
Flash memory inventor Fujio Masuoka received only $758,000 from Toshiba despite flash memory's massive industry impact
Modern storage class memory (PCM, STT-RAM, ReRAM) aims to bridge the gap between volatile RAM speed and non-volatile storage persistence
Energy-assisted magnetic recording (MAMR and HAMR) represents the frontier of hard disk technology, with HAMR requiring heating regions to 750°F in under a nanosecond
money_quotes: At this point in history, you could measure the speed of a storage algorithm by the distance the tape traveled.
Manufacturing rope memory was laborious, slow, and required skills analogous to textile work; it could take months to weave a program into the rope memory.

He sued Toshiba, demanding compensation for his work, which settled in 2006 with a one-time payment of ¥87m, equivalent to $758,000. This still seems light given how impactful flash memory has been on the industry.
If we compare NVMe to the 1951 UNISERVO metal tape, NVMe can read 486,111% more digits per second.
One thing that remains true is the storing of 0s and 1s. The means by which we do that vary greatly.
key_insight: While storage technology has advanced exponentially in speed and density over 70 years, the fundamental abstraction remains unchanged: everything reduces to representing and persisting 0s and 1s through whatever physical mechanism the era's technology enables.
